result:
The rain taps its Morse code against the windowpane, a quiet, insistent rhythm in the grey afternoon. I watch a single droplet trace a winding path down the glass, a miniature explorer navigating a transparent, vertical world. It hesitates, joins with another, and gains enough weight to plunge out of sight.

It makes me think of all the tiny, unobserved journeys happening all the time. The ant scaling a blade of grass, a dandelion seed caught in a thermal, the slow, stubborn creep of moss over a stone wall. We are so focused on our own grand narratives—the careers, the milestones, the headlines—that we miss the vast, silent symphony of small existences playing out just beside us.

There is a profound comfort in this. My worries, which felt so monolithic an hour ago, seem to shrink when held against the patient determination of a spider spinning its web in the eaves, a web destroyed by yesterday’s wind and rebuilt again today. The world is not asking for my drama. It is simply being: the
